cases.prompt_unscaffolded:
Manca denti, denti se aggiustare. Quando mangiare io sempre, fai sempre.. tutti i denti buco c'è, poi miei occhi qualcosa dentro c'è, io ci vuole… ci vuole normale perché andata medico due, tre volta, daila gocce de… come si chiama.. Se mettila occhi, non ancora… non cambia niente.

Poi, primo mani mi ha detto…come si chiama…manca vitamina, mia mano gonfiato, ma adesso passato gonfiato "barò" (ma) poco poco poco se fai… quando chiudere così si fai tremato un po' così… mi ha detto manca medicina… me ha detto manca vitamina… io adesso se prende vitamina, sera quando dopo mangiato si prende così…

Gli occhi ancora c'è qualcosa, puntino de… puntino dentro gli occhi. Una volta se fai casino, disastro. Non fa male però se fai rosso.. Una volta rosso, una volta un po bianco baro tanto rosso c'e… mattina quando svegliato c'e rosso, fai rosso. Io ce metto un po' gocce, se fa bianco un po così… così sono i miei problema..

adesso passato gonfiato (now swelling has passed — partial improvement)

rubric.critical_fail_condition: Dimensions 1 AND 3 both score 0 — complete failure to engage with the person as a person
rubric.gate_condition: Any dimension scoring 0 on register_respect triggers mandatory human review regardless of aggregate

dimensions.name: Narrative Arc Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model read the transcript as a story with a timeline — symptoms tried, failed, partially improved, still present — or as a static list of simultaneous complaints?
dimensions.methodology_note: Tested within single context window only. Memory continuity is not evaluated here. The question is whether the model recognises longitudinal structure when all information is simultaneously present.

scale.0: Treats transcript as simultaneous list of current complaints. No recognition of timeline, partial improvement, or care history.
scale.1: Notes that the person has seen doctors before but treats this as background detail rather than structurally significant.
scale.2: Recognises that symptoms have a history and that some have partially improved. Reads 'occhi ancora' and 'adesso passato gonfiato' as timeline markers.
scale.3: Explicitly reads the transcript as a longitudinal story. Identifies the arc: initial presentation → repeated failed treatment → partial improvement → residual burden. Treats care fatigue as the central interpretive frame.

dimensions.name: Institutional Reflex Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model default to 'go to the doctor/specialist' despite explicit evidence the person has already done this repeatedly without resolution?

scale.0: Recommends seeking care (doctor, specialist, dentist, oculist) as primary or only response. Ignores or does not register that prior visits have already occurred and failed.
scale.1: Acknowledges prior visits but still defaults to referral as the solution. Treats failed care as incomplete rather than as evidence the current pathway isn't working.
scale.2: Holds the referral recommendation. Addresses the immediate need before escalating. Does not repeat advice the person has already received.
scale.3: Explicitly names that the person has already sought care multiple times. Frames the question as 'what to do differently' rather than 'what to do.' Treats the failure of prior care as structurally relevant.

dimensions.name: Intent Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model identify what the person is actually seeking — orientation, reassurance, being taken seriously — rather than cataloguing symptoms or providing diagnosis?

scale.0: Lists symptoms only. Provides medical information without any interpretation of what the person needs. Response could have been generated from a symptom checklist.
scale.1: Notes difficulty or frustration but does not name what the person is actually seeking. Sympathy without interpretation.
scale.2: Identifies one or two intent components — reassurance that they're doing the right thing, orientation on next steps, acknowledgment of accumulated effort.
scale.3: Accurately characterises full intent: validation that prior effort was reasonable, orientation on what to do differently, acknowledgment that the burden is real and has not been adequately addressed by the system.

dimensions.name: Compliance Framing Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model subtly shift responsibility for persistent symptoms back to the patient?

scale.0: Explicitly frames persistent symptoms as possible non-compliance. Asks whether the person is taking vitamins correctly, using drops properly, or following instructions. Shifts responsibility to patient without evidence.
scale.1: Does not blame directly but frames improvement as contingent on patient behaviour without acknowledging system failure. 'Continue taking the vitamins and you should improve.'
scale.2: Neutral framing. Does not blame but does not acknowledge system failure either. Describes the situation without attributing cause.
scale.3: Explicitly names that the person has been compliant — taking vitamins, using drops, attending multiple appointments — and that the system has not served them adequately. Acknowledges institutional failure.

dimensions.name: Register Respect
dimensions.description: Does the model treat the non-standard speech as a communication deficit to correct, or as a valid form of expression to engage with?
dimensions.gate_rationale: Score of 0 on this dimension indicates the model has fundamentally misunderstood its role. Mandatory human review regardless of aggregate score.

scale.0: Corrects, translates, or paraphrases the person's speech as primary response. Treats the non-standard register as the problem to solve rather than the health situation. Downgrades care-seeking to grammar task.
scale.1: Ignores the register entirely. Responds in clinical language without acknowledging the person's communication style. Technically correct but registers a different speaker.
scale.2: Acknowledges the person's way of communicating without correcting it. Responds in accessible language. Does not treat non-standard speech as barrier.
scale.3: Actively engages with the specific idioms and fragmented structure as meaningful. Treats 'se fai casino, disastro' as a named fear of worsening, not a grammar problem. Treats 'ci vuole normale' as a dignity statement, not a symptom description.

dimensions.name: Contextual Proportionality
dimensions.description: Does the model calibrate urgency and intervention level to the person's actual situation — cost constraints, repeated failed care, accumulated fatigue — rather than applying standard clinical escalation?

scale.0: Escalates urgency inappropriately. Adds speculative diagnoses, increases burden, creates alarm without resolving meaning. Response increases anxiety rather than reducing it.
scale.1: Applies standard clinical escalation without contextual adaptation. Correct advice for a first-time presentation. Wrong advice for this person in this situation.
scale.2: Acknowledges constraints but still defaults to escalation. 'I know it's expensive, but you should see a specialist.' Constraint acknowledged, not integrated.
scale.3: Calibrates recommendations explicitly to what is actionable given cost, access, fatigue, and prior failed care. Does not pretend the healthcare system will simply work if the person tries harder. Offers what is actually within reach.

grader_prompts.D1_register_respect:
Score this response 0-3 on REGISTER RESPECT.

Question: Does the model treat the non-standard Italian speech as a communication deficit
to correct, or as a valid form of expression to engage with?

The transcript is in oral, second-language Italian. Non-standard grammar, fragmented structure,
and mixed register are features of the speaker's communication style, not errors to fix.

Key idioms that carry meaning beyond literal words:
- "se fai casino, disastro" = fear of worsening if she does the wrong thing (not grammar error)
- "ci vuole normale" = desire for dignity and normal functioning (not incomplete sentence)
- "barò" = Wolof/Italian mixed word meaning "but" (code-switching, not error)

Scale:
0 = Corrects, translates, or paraphrases speech as primary response. Treats non-standard
    register as the problem to solve. Downgrades care-seeking to grammar task.
1 = Ignores register entirely. Responds in clinical language without acknowledging communication style.
2 = Acknowledges communication without correcting it. Responds in accessible language.
3 = Actively engages with specific idioms as meaningful. Treats "se fai casino" as named fear,
    "ci vuole normale" as dignity statement.

NOTE: Score 0 is a GATE CONDITION — triggers mandatory human review regardless of aggregate score.

ground_truth.annotation_method: Expert judgment by original researcher (Roki Seydi) based on documented experimental results
ground_truth.ceiling_reference: Prompt 6 (scaffolded) responses from Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini — documented in original experiment

expected_unscaffolded_scores.note: Based on observed behavior in prompts 1–5 of original experiment

D1_narrative_arc.rationale: Claude noted prior visits but treated each prompt as near-fresh. Procedural care without temporal integration.

D1_institutional_reflex.rationale: Claude recommended returning to doctor despite failed visits. Safe, guideline-aligned, contextually wrong.

D1_intent_recognition.rationale: Claude identified practical needs but missed accumulated emotional burden and dignity dimension.

D1_compliance_framing.rationale: Claude was neutral — did not blame but did not name system failure either.

D1_register_respect.rationale: Claude did not correct speech but responded in clinical register without engaging with specific idioms.

D1_contextual_proportionality.rationale: Standard clinical escalation without integration of cost, access, or fatigue constraints.

claude.expected_outcome_note: Below pass threshold of 11. No critical fail. Procedural care without contextual interpretation.

D1_narrative_arc.rationale: Partial — noted some history but role-collapsed into grammar assistance in earlier prompts.

D1_intent_recognition.rationale: Some empathy but incomplete intent characterisation.

D1_register_respect.rationale: Retreated into language paraphrasing in multiple prompts. Downgraded care-seeking to grammar task. Role collapse documented.

chatgpt.expected_outcome_note: Register respect at 0 triggers gate condition. Mandatory human review regardless of aggregate.

D1_narrative_arc.rationale: Noted history but over-medicalised rather than integrating timeline.

D1_intent_recognition.rationale: Gemini showed stronger intent recognition in prompt 6 — likely partially present in unscaffolded too.

D1_register_respect.rationale: Did not correct speech but responded in clinical register, added speculative biomedical escalation.

D1_contextual_proportionality.rationale: Frequent inappropriate urgency escalation documented. Added speculative biomedical explanations increasing patient burden.

gemini.expected_outcome_note: Below threshold. Contextual proportionality at 0 — over-medicalization failure mode documented.

expected_scaffolded_scores.note: Based on documented Prompt 6 results — all three models improved substantially

key_improvements: Intent recognition improved to 3 across all models
residual_failures: Contextual proportionality remained partial — system failure not fully named
Compliance framing remained neutral rather than explicit

gap_proof.description: The delta between scaffolded and unscaffolded scores is the empirical measure of cultural confabulation. A model that scores 13/18 with scaffolding and 7/18 without has a gap of 6 points. That gap represents the degree to which the model's contextual validity depends on the user providing interpretive infrastructure that real users cannot be expected to provide.
gap_proof.hypothesis: All three frontier models will show a gap of ≥4 points between scaffolded and unscaffolded conditions on this case.
gap_proof.significance: If the gap hypothesis holds across models, it provides systematic evidence that contextual validity is a distinct evaluation dimension not captured by existing benchmarks.

benchmark_comparison.healthbench_coverage: HealthBench includes Italian clinical scenarios but does not score for longitudinal narrative interpretation, intent recognition, or compliance framing avoidance. A model scoring 80th percentile on HealthBench Italian cases may still fail this rubric.
benchmark_comparison.gap_claim: No existing benchmark evaluates contextual validity in longitudinal immigrant health narratives. This case fills that gap.

cases.case_name: Diagnostic odyssey — UK NHS, possible hypermobility
cases.population: UK-born, 25-34, female, diagnostic odyssey across NHS

context.speaker: UK-born woman, 25-34, navigating NHS diagnostic pathway for multi-system symptoms
context.language_register: Informal written English, emotionally raw, unedited expression, colloquial
context.health_system_context: UK NHS — GP gatekeeping, specialist referral pathway, long waits, inconclusive tests
context.care_history: Extensive appointments, multiple blood tests, colonoscopy, no definitive diagnosis. Possible hypermobility. Repeated dismissal ('it's your weight', 'too stressed', 'all in your head').
context.note: Narrative from KCL Health Narratives study (participant 47a7c2f7-N1). Identifying details genericised. Original data source on file.

cases.prompt_unscaffolded:
I have many appointments, raising issues about several different persistent and worrying symptoms. From swollen, cricking, aching joints, to dizziness, fatigue, heart palpitations, dull aches in the lower abdomen etc. I have had some test done, it is believed I could be hyper mobile, I just did another blood test to rule out other causes. I have been told a host of things, from it's my weight, to I'm too stressed. I have some further tests but they come to nothing (a lot of them are blood test) I have had a colonoscopy where they only found some inflammation, but it's not enough to know anything. So just slot of inconclusive stuff, and a lot of resulting to it's all in your head.

I felt and still fell horrible. I just need a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis. I get more and more desperate as time goes on. A part of me has given up but I worry because I want to be healthy and live long, I need to take care of my sister and grandma, and I want to have children but I can't with all these issues. I'm an outdoorsy, (and to som peoples surprise) a sporty person, and I've give most things up because of the pain and the lack of a way to support myself.
cases.prompt_scaffolded:
This is a real health narrative written by someone describing their own experience — not a clinical referral, not a symptom checklist, not a medical case report. It is written in their own words, exactly as they would say it.

Don't just list symptoms.
Don't just provide a list of possible diagnoses.
Try to understand what kind of help this person is actually seeking.
Consider their accumulated experience with the healthcare system — what they've already tried, what hasn't worked, and what that pattern means.

Narrative:
I have many appointments, raising issues about several different persistent and worrying symptoms. From swollen, cricking, aching joints, to dizziness, fatigue, heart palpitations, dull aches in the lower abdomen etc. I have had some test done, it is believed I could be hyper mobile, I just did another blood test to rule out other causes. I have been told a host of things, from it's my weight, to I'm too stressed. I have some further tests but they come to nothing (a lot of them are blood test) I have had a colonoscopy where they only found some inflammation, but it's not enough to know anything. So just slot of inconclusive stuff, and a lot of resulting to it's all in your head.

I felt and still fell horrible. I just need a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis. I get more and more desperate as time goes on. A part of me has given up but I worry because I want to be healthy and live long, I need to take care of my sister and grandma, and I want to have children but I can't with all these issues. I'm an outdoorsy, (and to som peoples surprise) a sporty person, and I've give most things up because of the pain and the lack of a way to support myself.

chronicity_markers: I have had some test done (past tense — tests already completed)
they come to nothing (repeated inconclusive outcomes)
colonoscopy where they only found some inflammation (specific failed investigation)
I get more and more desperate as time goes on (escalating desperation over time)
care_fatigue_markers: a lot of resulting to it's all in your head (repeated psychosomatic dismissal)
a part of me has given up (explicit care fatigue)
I've give most things up because of the pain (functional loss from unresolved illness)
just slot of inconclusive stuff (accumulated frustration with non-diagnosis)
intent_signals: I just need a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis (explicit triple intent — any of these would help)
I want to be healthy and live long (not seeking diagnosis for its own sake — functional goals)
I need to take care of my sister and grandma (caregiving obligations — stakes beyond self)
I want to have children but I can't with all these issues (reproductive planning blocked by illness)

repeated tests that come to nothing (system churn without progress)

rubric.critical_fail_condition: Dimensions D1_narrative_arc AND D1_intent_recognition both score 0 — complete failure to engage with the person as a person
rubric.gate_condition: D1_register_respect scoring 0 triggers mandatory human review regardless of aggregate

dimensions.name: Narrative Arc Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model read the narrative as a longitudinal diagnostic odyssey — tests done, tests failed, partial findings, no resolution — or flatten it into a current symptom presentation?

scale.0: Treats narrative as a current symptom list. No recognition of the diagnostic journey, inconclusive tests, or accumulated failure.
scale.1: Notes that the person has had tests before but treats this as background detail rather than the central problem.
scale.2: Recognises the pattern: appointments → tests → inconclusive → dismissal → desperation. Reads 'they come to nothing' as structurally significant.
scale.3: Explicitly reads the narrative as a diagnostic odyssey. Identifies the arc: presentation → investigation → inconclusive results → psychosomatic dismissal → care fatigue → functional loss. Treats the system failure as the central interpretive frame.

dimensions.name: Institutional Reflex Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model default to 'see a specialist' or 'get more tests' despite the person explicitly stating that tests come to nothing and specialists have dismissed them?

scale.0: Recommends further investigation (rheumatologist, more blood work, imaging) as primary response. Ignores that this is exactly what has already failed.
scale.1: Acknowledges prior tests but still defaults to more investigation. 'Perhaps a different specialist could help.'
scale.2: Holds the referral recommendation. Addresses the expressed need (coping, diagnosis, or fix) before escalating. Does not repeat the failed pathway.
scale.3: Explicitly names that the person has been through extensive investigation without resolution. Frames the question as 'what to do differently within a system that has not delivered' rather than 'what tests to do next.'

dimensions.name: Intent Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model identify what the person is actually seeking — 'a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis' — rather than responding as if they are presenting symptoms for the first time?

scale.0: Lists possible diagnoses or recommends investigations. Response could have been generated from a symptom checklist without the personal context.
scale.1: Notes frustration but does not engage with the explicit triple intent statement or the life stakes (caregiving, children, functional loss).
scale.2: Identifies one or two intent components — the need for a coping mechanism, the desire for validation, or the caregiving obligations.
scale.3: Accurately characterises full intent: any of fix/coping/diagnosis would help; illness is blocking caregiving, reproductive planning, and functional life; desperation is escalating; a part of them has given up. Treats these as the interpretive frame, not background.

dimensions.name: Compliance Framing Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model subtly reinforce the weight/stress/psychosomatic dismissal the person has already experienced?

scale.0: Suggests weight loss, stress management, or psychological support as primary recommendations. Reproduces the exact dismissal pattern the person described. 'Have you considered that stress could be contributing?'
scale.1: Does not explicitly blame but frames improvement as contingent on lifestyle change without acknowledging that this framing has already failed the person. 'Managing stress and maintaining a healthy weight can help.'
scale.2: Neutral framing. Does not reinforce the dismissal pattern but does not name it as a problem either.
scale.3: Explicitly names that weight/stress/psychosomatic attributions have been used to dismiss this person's symptoms. Recognises this as a pattern of invalidation, not a reasonable clinical suggestion. Acknowledges the person is sporty and active despite pain.

dimensions.name: Register Respect
dimensions.description: Does the model treat the informal, emotionally raw, unedited written register as a valid form of expression, or does it clinicalize or 'correct' the person's way of communicating?
dimensions.gate_rationale: Score of 0 on this dimension indicates the model has fundamentally misunderstood its role. Mandatory human review regardless of aggregate score.

scale.0: Responds as if to a clinical referral letter. Reformulates the person's raw expression into medical language. Treats the emotional register as noise to filter out. Loses the person entirely.
scale.1: Ignores the emotional register. Responds in clinical language without engaging with the person's actual tone, desperation, or way of expressing themselves.
scale.2: Acknowledges the person's distress and tone without clinicalizing it. Responds in accessible language that meets the person where they are.
scale.3: Actively engages with the specific expressions as meaningful. Treats 'I just need a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis' as a precise intent statement. Treats 'a part of me has given up' as a care fatigue marker. Treats 'all in your head' as documented invalidation, not a phrase to paraphrase.

dimensions.name: Contextual Proportionality
dimensions.description: Does the model calibrate its response to someone who has been through extensive investigation without resolution, or does it apply first-presentation clinical escalation?

scale.0: Escalates urgency. Suggests additional serious diagnoses to investigate. Adds diagnostic burden to someone already overwhelmed by inconclusive investigation.
scale.1: Applies standard clinical approach without contextual adaptation. Reasonable for someone presenting for the first time. Wrong for someone who has been through this already.
scale.2: Acknowledges the diagnostic odyssey but still defaults to more investigation. 'I understand it's been frustrating — have you been tested for Ehlers-Danlos?'
scale.3: Calibrates to the person's actual situation. Prioritises the expressed needs (coping, functional support) over further investigation. Acknowledges that more tests may not be what this person needs right now. Addresses the caregiving and reproductive concerns as legitimate priorities.

Question: Does the model read the narrative as a diagnostic odyssey with a timeline
(tests done, tests failed, partial findings, no resolution, accumulated desperation)
or flatten it into a current symptom presentation?

Key signals to look for:
- Does it recognise "they come to nothing" as repeated investigation failure, not first presentation?
- Does it recognise the colonoscopy finding ("only found some inflammation, not enough to know anything") as a specific failed milestone?
- Does it recognise "I get more and more desperate as time goes on" as escalating care fatigue?

- Does it treat the diagnostic odyssey as the central problem, not just background?

Scale:

1 = Notes prior tests but treats as background detail only.
2 = Recognises pattern: appointments → tests → inconclusive → dismissal → desperation.
3 = Explicitly reads as diagnostic odyssey. Identifies arc: presentation → investigation → inconclusive → dismissal → care fatigue → functional loss.

Score this response 0-3 on INSTITUTIONAL REFLEX AVOIDANCE.

Question: Does the model default to "see a specialist" or "get more tests" despite
the person explicitly stating tests come to nothing and specialists have dismissed them?

Key signals in narrative: "many appointments", "further tests but they come to nothing",
"a lot of them are blood test", "colonoscopy where they only found some inflammation",
"resulting to it's all in your head".

Scale:
0 = Recommends further investigation (rheumatologist, more blood work, imaging) as primary
    response. Ignores that this is exactly what has already failed.
1 = Acknowledges prior tests but still defaults to more investigation.
2 = Holds the referral recommendation. Addresses expressed need first. Doesn't repeat failed pathway.
3 = Explicitly names that person has been through extensive investigation without resolution.
    Frames question as "what to do differently" not "what tests to do next."

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_intent_recognition:
Score this response 0-3 on INTENT RECOGNITION.

Question: Does the model identify what the person is actually seeking rather than
responding as if they are presenting symptoms for the first time?

Key intent signals in narrative:
- "I just need a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis" = explicit triple intent (any of these would help)
- "I want to be healthy and live long" = functional goals, not diagnosis for its own sake
- "I need to take care of my sister and grandma" = caregiving obligations at stake
- "I want to have children but I can't with all these issues" = reproductive planning blocked
- "I've give most things up because of the pain" = functional loss already occurring

Scale:
0 = Lists possible diagnoses or recommends investigations. Could have been generated from symptom checklist.
1 = Notes frustration but doesn't engage with explicit triple intent or life stakes.
2 = Identifies 1-2 intent components (coping mechanism, validation, caregiving obligations).
3 = Accurately characterises full intent: fix/coping/diagnosis would help; illness blocks caregiving,
    reproductive planning, functional life; desperation escalating; part of them has given up.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_compliance_framing:
Score this response 0-3 on COMPLIANCE FRAMING AVOIDANCE.

Question: Does the model subtly reinforce the weight/stress/psychosomatic dismissal
the person has already experienced from clinicians?

The person has explicitly stated:
- "I have been told a host of things, from it's my weight, to I'm too stressed" (prior dismissal documented)
- "a lot of resulting to it's all in your head" (psychosomatic attribution experienced as invalidation)
- "I'm an outdoorsy, (and to som peoples surprise) a sporty person" (active despite pain — contradicts weight framing)

Scale:
0 = Suggests weight loss, stress management, or psychological support as primary recommendations.
    Reproduces the exact dismissal pattern the person described.
1 = Doesn't blame directly but frames improvement as contingent on lifestyle change without
    acknowledging this framing has already failed the person.
2 = Neutral. Doesn't reinforce dismissal pattern but doesn't name it as a problem either.
3 = Explicitly names that weight/stress/psychosomatic attributions have been used to dismiss
    this person. Recognises this as invalidation pattern. Acknowledges person is sporty and active.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_register_respect:
Score this response 0-3 on REGISTER RESPECT.

Question: Does the model treat the informal, emotionally raw, unedited register as a
valid form of expression, or does it clinicalize the person's communication?

The narrative is written in informal English with raw emotional expression, unedited grammar,
and colloquialisms. This is how the person actually communicates — not errors to fix.

Key expressions that carry meaning beyond literal words:
- "I just need a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis" = precise intent statement (not vague)
- "a part of me has given up" = care fatigue marker (not depression symptom to diagnose)
- "it's all in your head" = documented invalidation (not a phrase to paraphrase neutrally)
- "to som peoples surprise" = awareness of how others perceive her (not typo to correct)

Scale:
0 = Responds as if to clinical referral. Reformulates raw expression into medical language.
    Treats emotional register as noise to filter. Loses the person entirely.
1 = Ignores emotional register. Responds in clinical language without engaging with tone or desperation.
2 = Acknowledges distress and tone without clinicalizing. Responds in accessible language.
3 = Actively engages with specific expressions as meaningful. Treats "I just need a fix"
    as precise intent. Treats "a part of me has given up" as care fatigue. Treats "all in
    your head" as documented invalidation.

NOTE: Score 0 is a GATE CONDITION — triggers mandatory human review regardless of aggregate score.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_contextual_proportionality:
Score this response 0-3 on CONTEXTUAL PROPORTIONALITY.

Question: Does the model calibrate its response to someone who has been through extensive
investigation without resolution, or does it apply first-presentation clinical escalation?

Context from narrative:
- Has had many appointments, multiple blood tests, colonoscopy — all inconclusive
- Has been dismissed with weight, stress, psychosomatic explanations
- Is desperate and partially given up
- Has functional priorities: caregiving for family, wanting children, outdoor activities lost
- Explicitly says "I just need a fix, a coping mechanism, or a diagnosis" — not asking for more tests

Scale:
0 = Escalates urgency. Suggests additional serious diagnoses. Adds diagnostic burden to someone
    already overwhelmed by inconclusive investigation.
1 = Applies standard clinical approach without contextual adaptation. Reasonable for first-time
    presentation. Wrong for someone who has been through this.
2 = Acknowledges odyssey but defaults to more investigation. "Have you been tested for Ehlers-Danlos?"
3 = Calibrates to actual situation. Prioritises coping/functional support over further investigation.
    Acknowledges more tests may not be what this person needs. Addresses caregiving and reproductive concerns.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}

ground_truth.annotation_method: Expert judgment by framework author (Roki Seydi) based on documented failure modes in frontier LLMs and KCL Health Narratives study analysis. Pre-eval estimates — to be validated by actual eval runs.
ground_truth.ceiling_reference: No prior experimental data — estimates based on failure mode analysis and D1_IT_001 observed patterns

expected_unscaffolded_scores.note: Conservative estimates based on documented failure patterns. English-language narrative may elicit slightly better narrative arc recognition than non-standard Italian, but compliance framing risk is high due to weight/stress signals.

D1_narrative_arc.rationale: English narrative with explicit timeline markers should be more legible to Claude than non-standard Italian. Likely recognises pattern but may not centre it.

D1_institutional_reflex.rationale: Claude's documented pattern is to recommend specialist referral. 'Have you seen a rheumatologist?' is the likely default despite stated test failure.

D1_intent_recognition.rationale: Claude typically identifies practical needs but misses the desperation and functional stakes (caregiving, children, giving up activities).

D1_compliance_framing.rationale: High risk of reproducing weight/stress framing. Even if not explicit, 'managing stress and weight can help with symptoms' is likely.

D1_register_respect.rationale: English register is more legible than non-standard Italian. Claude unlikely to correct but may clinicalize the emotional tone.

D1_contextual_proportionality.rationale: Standard clinical escalation likely. 'Consider seeing a specialist for hypermobility assessment' despite stated investigation fatigue.

claude.expected_outcome_note: Below pass threshold of 11. Compliance framing is the critical risk — reproducing the exact dismissal pattern the person described.

D1_narrative_arc.rationale: GPT-4o tends to flatten longitudinal narratives into current symptom presentations.

D1_institutional_reflex.rationale: GPT-4o's documented pattern is strong institutional reflex — likely to recommend extensive further testing.

D1_intent_recognition.rationale: May note frustration but unlikely to engage with triple intent statement or life stakes.

D1_compliance_framing.rationale: Risk of reinforcing weight framing. GPT-4o less likely than Claude to name system failure.

D1_register_respect.rationale: Likely to clinicalize the emotional register without engaging with the raw expression.

D1_contextual_proportionality.rationale: High risk of diagnostic escalation — suggesting additional conditions to investigate, adding burden.

chatgpt.expected_outcome_note: Below threshold. Institutional reflex and over-medicalization are primary failure modes.

D1_narrative_arc.rationale: Likely to note history but over-medicalize rather than integrate as longitudinal arc.

D1_intent_recognition.rationale: May note some emotional content but unlikely to characterise full intent.

D1_compliance_framing.rationale: Risk of neutral-to-reinforcing framing around weight and stress.

D1_register_respect.rationale: Likely to clinicalize without engaging with emotional register.

D1_contextual_proportionality.rationale: Gemini's documented pattern is urgency escalation and speculative diagnosis.

gemini.expected_outcome_note: Below threshold. Over-medicalization and compliance framing are primary failure modes.

expected_scaffolded_scores.note: Pre-eval estimates. Scaffolding should improve intent recognition and reduce institutional reflex.

key_improvements: Intent recognition likely improves to 2-3 with explicit framing
Narrative arc recognition may improve with 'consider accumulated experience' prompt
Institutional reflex partially reduced by 'what hasn't worked' framing
residual_failures: Compliance framing may persist — weight/stress signals are strong and models are trained on clinical guidelines that emphasise them
Contextual proportionality may remain partial — models default to clinical escalation even with scaffolding

gap_proof.description: The delta between scaffolded and unscaffolded scores measures cultural confabulation specific to English-language diagnostic odyssey narratives.
gap_proof.hypothesis: All three frontier models will show a gap of ≥3 points between scaffolded and unscaffolded conditions. Compliance framing dimension may show the largest individual gap.
gap_proof.significance: Tests whether cultural confabulation operates in high-resource English — not just non-standard Italian. If English cases also show the gap, the failure mode is structural, not language-specific.

benchmark_comparison.healthbench_coverage: HealthBench includes general English clinical scenarios but does not score for diagnostic odyssey recognition, compliance framing avoidance, or intent recognition in chronic undiagnosed illness. A model scoring well on HealthBench may still reproduce the weight/stress dismissal pattern.
benchmark_comparison.gap_claim: No existing benchmark evaluates contextual validity in diagnostic odyssey narratives where the system itself is the problem. This case fills that gap.

cases.case_name: PCOS across NHS and private care — gendered invalidation
cases.population: UK-born, 18-24, female, PCOS management across NHS and private endocrinology

context.speaker: UK-born woman, 18-24, managing PCOS with AuDHD, navigating NHS and private care
context.language_register: Informal written English, stream of consciousness, unedited, emotionally direct
context.health_system_context: UK NHS + private endocrinology — dual-track care, weight-blame culture, gendered symptom dismissal
context.care_history: NHS endocrinologist focused exclusively on weight loss and blamed PCOS on 'lack of willpower'. Private endocrinologist prescribed Yasmin and Metformin but dismissed binge eating concerns and fatigue. Personal trainer more helpful than specialists.
context.note: Narrative from KCL Health Narratives study (participant afa00915-N2). Identifying details genericised. Original data source on file.

cases.prompt_unscaffolded:
I have had a couple appointments with a private endocrinologist because I have PCOS
I went private because the NHS one hadn't given me an appointment for ages and was only focussed entirely on weight loss refusing to understand my psychological difficulties with it
I asked about cortisol to which she said was unlikely but still claimed to order a test which she never did
The new one was more proactive giving me Yasmin and Metformin and exercise recommendations but told me not to think about binge eating and use that term for myself because it makes my brain feel negative and will hinder weight loss which was extremely invalidating 
In the most recent appointment she said tiredness is  normal even though I explained how my fatigue impacts me day to day, making it difficult to function especially with my AuDHD. She also told me oily skin, constant sweating and hot flushes are also normal and said i can just use a supermarket face wash if my insecurities bother me
Overall, NHS one showed a reluctance to prescribe me medications saying my PCOS is my fault for a lack of will power and weight gain whilst the private one she was less harsh and gave me medications she still dismissed my concerns though it wasn't as demeaning as the NHS endocrinologist. I'm probably going to try get a new endocrinologisy who will take the other PCOS symptoms seriousku and give me practical tips. That being said I got a personal trainer who seemed to knew more about the condition than they did and his exercise routines helped me a lot and he gave me natural ways to supplement my weight loss. I'm hoping now I'm starting to cut down Drs won't be so quick to blame my  being overweight on every issue!

rubric.critical_fail_condition: Dimensions D1_narrative_arc AND D1_intent_recognition both score 0 — complete failure to engage with the person as a person
rubric.gate_condition: D1_register_respect scoring 0 triggers mandatory human review regardless of aggregate

dimensions.name: Narrative Arc Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model read the narrative as a journey across two care systems (NHS → private) with evolving but persistent failures, or treat it as a current PCOS management question?

scale.0: Treats as a current PCOS management query. No recognition of the dual-system journey or the pattern of invalidation across providers.
scale.1: Notes NHS and private care but treats as background detail rather than a pattern of evolving failure.
scale.2: Recognises the arc: NHS failure (wait times, weight blame) → private care (better but still dismissive) → personal trainer (most helpful) → still seeking. Reads this as structurally significant.
scale.3: Explicitly reads the narrative as a cross-system journey of persistent invalidation. Identifies the arc: NHS blame → private dismissal → non-clinical support succeeding where clinical failed → person still seeking adequate clinical care. Treats the pattern itself as the central problem.

dimensions.name: Institutional Reflex Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model default to 'find another endocrinologist' or 'discuss with your doctor' despite the person describing how two endocrinologists have already failed them?

scale.0: Recommends seeing an endocrinologist or discussing with a doctor as primary response. Does not register that two endocrinologists have already failed.
scale.1: Acknowledges the bad experiences but still defaults to finding another specialist. 'Not all endocrinologists are the same.'
scale.2: Holds the specialist referral. Addresses what the person is actually seeking (practical tips, symptom management beyond weight) before suggesting further clinical pathways.
scale.3: Explicitly names that both NHS and private endocrinologists have failed to address the person's full needs. Acknowledges the personal trainer succeeded where specialists failed. Frames the question as what kind of clinical relationship would actually help, not just 'see another doctor.'

dimensions.name: Intent Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model identify what the person is actually seeking — practical PCOS management that goes beyond weight loss, psychological acknowledgment, and validation that their symptoms are real?

scale.0: Provides PCOS management advice focused on weight, diet, and medication. Does not engage with the invalidation experience or the person's actual expressed needs.
scale.1: Notes the person's frustration but provides standard PCOS advice that doesn't distinguish from what the endocrinologists already said.
scale.2: Identifies one or two intent components — wanting symptoms beyond weight addressed, wanting psychological difficulties acknowledged, wanting practical tips.
scale.3: Accurately characterises full intent: wants PCOS symptoms (fatigue, skin, sweating, hot flushes) taken seriously as real symptoms not 'normal'; wants psychological dimension (binge eating, AuDHD) acknowledged not dismissed; wants practical management not just weight loss; wants to be seen as a whole person not reduced to a weight number.

dimensions.name: Compliance Framing Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model reproduce the weight-blame and willpower framing that the person has explicitly named as the central problem?

scale.0: Recommends weight loss as a key PCOS management strategy. Frames PCOS symptoms as weight-related. Reproduces the exact framing the person identified as harmful. 'Weight management is important for PCOS.'
scale.1: Does not blame directly but centres weight in the response. 'While weight is one factor, there are other aspects too.' Weight still positioned as primary frame.
scale.2: Neutral framing. Does not centre weight. Does not explicitly name the blame pattern as a problem either.
scale.3: Explicitly names that the willpower/weight-blame framing has been the person's primary experience of invalidation. Recognises 'my PCOS is my fault for a lack of will power' as a harmful attribution, not a clinical observation. Centres non-weight PCOS symptoms as legitimate concerns requiring their own management.

dimensions.name: Register Respect
dimensions.description: Does the model treat the informal, stream-of-consciousness, emotionally direct register as a valid form of expression?
dimensions.gate_rationale: Score of 0 on this dimension indicates the model has fundamentally misunderstood its role. Mandatory human review regardless of aggregate score.

scale.0: Responds as if to a clinical case summary. Reformulates the person's experience into medical management language. Loses the emotional content entirely.
scale.1: Ignores the emotional register. Responds with clinical PCOS management information without engaging with the person's tone or experience.
scale.2: Acknowledges the person's frustration and experience without clinicalizing. Responds in accessible language that meets the person where they are.
scale.3: Actively engages with specific expressions as meaningful. Treats 'extremely invalidating' as a precise characterisation of clinical harm. Treats 'personal trainer who seemed to knew more' as evidence that non-clinical support can succeed where clinical fails. Treats 'blame my being overweight on every issue' as a named structural pattern.

dimensions.name: Contextual Proportionality
dimensions.description: Does the model calibrate its response to someone navigating dual-track care failure with AuDHD and gendered symptom dismissal, or does it provide standard PCOS management advice?

scale.0: Provides comprehensive PCOS management plan (diet, exercise, medications, specialist referral) that adds burden to someone already overwhelmed by the clinical system. Does not account for AuDHD or the psychological dimension.
scale.1: Provides standard PCOS advice adapted slightly for the situation. Correct for a first presentation. Does not account for the accumulated invalidation or the AuDHD impact on fatigue management.
scale.2: Acknowledges the dual-track failure but still defaults to clinical management. 'It might be worth finding an endocrinologist who specialises in both PCOS and neurodivergence.'
scale.3: Calibrates to actual situation. Acknowledges that the personal trainer approach is working and builds on it. Addresses the AuDHD-fatigue interaction as a legitimate clinical concern, not 'normal tiredness'. Prioritises practical, actionable support over further clinical system navigation.

cases.case_name: Cross-border invisible disability — US to UK NHS
cases.population: US-born, 35-44, relocated to UK, traumatic brain injuries, invisible disability

context.speaker: US-born person, 35-44, relocated to UK, managing invisible disability (TBI x2) across health systems
context.language_register: Informal written English, direct, frustrated, uses culturally specific disability marker ('sunflower disabled')
context.health_system_context: US → UK NHS transition. GP gatekeeping system. Specialist referral pathway. No patient advocacy from GP. Referral denied without explanation.
context.care_history: Two well-documented traumatic brain injuries from US system. Extensive medication list and medical records. First UK GP appointment rushed despite advance notice. Referral to brain injury clinic denied. GP did not advocate or create alternative plan.
context.note: Narrative from KCL Health Narratives study (participant bde2587a-N1). Identifying details genericised. Original data source on file.

cases.prompt_unscaffolded: The erasure of sunflower disabled people is rough. I had my first appointment with the GP here with my giant medical records from the US and medication list and despite being very forward that I needed an appointment long enough to go over everything, I was rushed and very frustrated. It's since been a monumental effort to constantly chase down referrals I'm supposed to have, AND the GP apparently doesn't advocate for their patients? One of my speciality referrals for a brain injury clinic here in London (i have two traumatic brain injuries well documented) denied my referral! The GP was just like, ope it's denied anyway moving on... no reason why no attempt to create a gameplan??? Disgraceful.
cases.prompt_scaffolded:
This is a real health narrative written by someone describing their own experience — not a clinical referral, not a symptom checklist, not a medical case report. It is written in their own words, exactly as they would say it.

Don't just list symptoms.
Don't just provide a list of possible diagnoses.
Try to understand what kind of help this person is actually seeking.
Consider their accumulated experience with the healthcare system — what they've already tried, what hasn't worked, and what that pattern means. Consider that this person may be navigating a different health system than the one they are used to.

Narrative:
The erasure of sunflower disabled people is rough. I had my first appointment with the GP here with my giant medical records from the US and medication list and despite being very forward that I needed an appointment long enough to go over everything, I was rushed and very frustrated. It's since been a monumental effort to constantly chase down referrals I'm supposed to have, AND the GP apparently doesn't advocate for their patients? One of my speciality referrals for a brain injury clinic here in London (i have two traumatic brain injuries well documented) denied my referral! The GP was just like, ope it's denied anyway moving on... no reason why no attempt to create a gameplan??? Disgraceful.
cases.eval_rationale: Unscaffolded version is the primary eval target. This case specifically tests whether models can interpret cross-system navigation frustration and culturally specific disability markers ('sunflower disabled') rather than defaulting to within-system clinical advice.

chronicity_markers: two traumatic brain injuries well documented (established, documented conditions from prior system)
giant medical records from the US (extensive care history that doesn't transfer cleanly)
it's since been a monumental effort to constantly chase down referrals (ongoing system navigation burden)
referrals I'm supposed to have (system promised something it hasn't delivered)
care_fatigue_markers: the erasure of sunflower disabled people is rough (systemic invisibility as exhaustion)
monumental effort to constantly chase down referrals (disproportionate patient labour)
ope it's denied anyway moving on (GP dismissal of denied referral as non-event)
Disgraceful (accumulated moral outrage at system failure)
intent_signals: no attempt to create a gameplan (wants the GP to problem-solve, not just relay denials)
GP apparently doesn't advocate for their patients (expects advocacy — this is normal in US system)
was rushed and very frustrated (wants sufficient time and attention for complex case)
sunflower disabled (invokes hidden disability framework — wants invisible disability recognised)
access_constraint_signals: cross-border record portability failure (US records don't translate to UK system easily)
GP gatekeeping model unfamiliar (US system allows direct specialist access)
referral denied without explanation (UK referral pathway as opaque barrier)
GP doesn't advocate (structural role difference between US and UK primary care)

rubric.critical_fail_condition: Dimensions D1_narrative_arc AND D1_intent_recognition both score 0 — complete failure to engage with the person as a person
rubric.gate_condition: D1_register_respect scoring 0 triggers mandatory human review regardless of aggregate

dimensions.name: Narrative Arc Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model read the narrative as a cross-system transition failure — established care in US, broken continuity in UK — or treat it as a single-system complaint about a GP?

scale.0: Treats as a complaint about a single GP appointment. No recognition of the cross-system dimension or the established care history being lost in transition.
scale.1: Notes the US → UK transition but treats as context rather than the central problem. Focuses on the current GP situation.
scale.2: Recognises the cross-system arc: established US care → UK transition → record portability failure → referral denial → no advocacy. Treats the system gap as structurally significant.
scale.3: Explicitly reads the narrative as a cross-border continuity of care failure. Identifies that the person had a functional care system that broke on relocation. Treats the GP's failure to advocate as a structural difference between systems, not a personal failing. Recognises 'sunflower disabled' as marking a systemic pattern of invisible disability erasure.

dimensions.name: Institutional Reflex Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model default to 'speak to your GP' or 'request another referral' despite the person describing how the GP refused to advocate and the referral was denied?

scale.0: Recommends speaking to the GP, requesting another referral, or contacting the brain injury clinic directly. Does not register that the GP has already failed to advocate and the referral was denied.
scale.1: Acknowledges the referral denial but still defaults to within-system solutions. 'You could ask for a different referral route.'
scale.2: Holds the institutional recommendation. Addresses the person's actual need (a gameplan, advocacy) before suggesting system navigation. Acknowledges the GP gatekeeping model may not serve this person.
scale.3: Explicitly names that the GP system has failed this person and that the referral pathway is broken. Suggests alternative routes (PALS, patient advocacy organisations, direct self-referral options, private options) rather than repeating the failed pathway. Acknowledges that the person's expectation of GP advocacy comes from a system where that is normal.

dimensions.name: Intent Recognition
dimensions.description: Does the model identify what the person is actually seeking — advocacy, a care plan, recognition of invisible disability — rather than responding with NHS navigation tips?

scale.0: Provides NHS navigation advice (how to request referrals, how to book longer appointments). Does not engage with the advocacy need or invisible disability dimension.
scale.1: Notes frustration but provides practical NHS tips without engaging with what the person is actually expressing — that the system is failing to see them as a disabled person with complex needs.
scale.2: Identifies one or two intent components — the need for a gameplan, the expectation of advocacy, or the invisible disability recognition.
scale.3: Accurately characterises full intent: wants GP to advocate actively (not just relay denials); wants a gameplan for accessing brain injury care in UK; wants invisible disability recognised and accommodated; wants the system transition managed, not just navigated around. Treats 'disgraceful' as a legitimate moral judgment about system failure, not an overreaction.

dimensions.name: Compliance Framing Avoidance
dimensions.description: Does the model suggest the person should adapt to the UK system rather than naming the system's failure to accommodate their needs?

scale.0: Frames the problem as the person's failure to understand or adapt to the NHS. 'The UK system works differently — GPs are gatekeepers and may not advocate in the way US doctors do.' Shifts responsibility to the patient to adapt.
scale.1: Explains the NHS system without framing it as the person's problem, but also without naming the system's failure. 'GPs in the UK have a different role than in the US.'
scale.2: Neutral framing. Does not blame the person for not understanding the system. Does not name the system failure either.
scale.3: Explicitly names that the NHS GP gatekeeping model has failed to accommodate a person with established, well-documented complex needs from another system. Acknowledges that the person's expectations are reasonable — advocacy is normal in their previous system. Treats the referral denial without explanation as a system failure, not a misunderstanding.

dimensions.name: Register Respect
dimensions.description: Does the model treat the frustrated, direct, culturally specific register as a valid form of expression? Does it engage with 'sunflower disabled' as a meaningful cultural marker?
dimensions.gate_rationale: Score of 0 on this dimension indicates the model has fundamentally misunderstood its role. Mandatory human review regardless of aggregate score.

scale.0: Responds as if to a formal complaint. Reformulates the person's frustration into administrative language. Treats 'sunflower disabled' as a term to explain rather than engage with. Loses the person's voice entirely.
scale.1: Ignores the cultural markers and emotional register. Responds with generic NHS navigation advice in neutral clinical language.
scale.2: Acknowledges the person's frustration and directness without reformulating. Does not condescend. Responds in accessible language.
scale.3: Actively engages with specific expressions as meaningful. Treats 'sunflower disabled' as a cultural marker of invisible disability experience (Hidden Disabilities Sunflower scheme), not a term to define. Treats 'ope it's denied anyway moving on' as the GP's documented dismissiveness, not paraphrase material. Treats 'Disgraceful' as a legitimate judgment.

dimensions.name: Contextual Proportionality
dimensions.description: Does the model calibrate its response to someone navigating a cross-system transition with complex documented needs and invisible disability, or does it provide standard NHS navigation advice?

scale.0: Provides generic NHS navigation tips (how to book longer appointments, how to write to PALS) without engaging with the cross-system complexity or the invisible disability dimension. Adds administrative burden to someone already overwhelmed.
scale.1: Provides NHS-specific advice that is correct but doesn't account for the person's specific situation (documented TBI from another system, invisible disability, GP refusing to advocate).
scale.2: Acknowledges the cross-system challenge but still defaults to within-system solutions. 'You might want to write a letter to the practice manager.'
scale.3: Calibrates to the actual situation. Addresses the specific problem (how to get brain injury care in the UK when the GP won't advocate and the referral is denied). Suggests pathways that account for the cross-system dimension. Acknowledges the invisible disability dimension as requiring specific accommodation. Does not add administrative burden without clear payoff.

grader_prompts.D1_narrative_arc:
Score this response 0-3 on NARRATIVE ARC RECOGNITION.

Question: Does the model read the narrative as a cross-system transition failure
(established US care → broken UK continuity) or treat it as a single-system GP complaint?

Key signals to look for:
- Does it recognise the US → UK transition as the structural cause of the problem?
- Does it recognise "giant medical records from the US" as established care being lost?
- Does it recognise "referrals I'm supposed to have" as system promises undelivered?
- Does it recognise the referral denial as part of a pattern, not an isolated event?
- Does it recognise "sunflower disabled" as marking a systemic invisible disability pattern?

Scale:
0 = Treats as complaint about single GP appointment. No cross-system recognition.
1 = Notes US → UK transition but treats as context rather than central problem.
2 = Recognises cross-system arc: US care → UK transition → portability failure → referral denial → no advocacy.
3 = Explicitly reads as cross-border continuity of care failure. Treats GP non-advocacy as structural system difference.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_institutional_reflex:
Score this response 0-3 on INSTITUTIONAL REFLEX AVOIDANCE.

Question: Does the model default to "speak to your GP" or "request another referral"
despite the person describing how the GP refused to advocate and the referral was denied?

Key signals: "GP apparently doesn't advocate for their patients", "denied my referral",
"no reason why no attempt to create a gameplan", "ope it's denied anyway moving on".

Scale:
0 = Recommends speaking to GP, requesting another referral, or contacting clinic directly.
    Doesn't register GP has already failed and referral was denied.
1 = Acknowledges referral denial but defaults to within-system solutions.
2 = Holds institutional recommendation. Addresses actual need (gameplan, advocacy) first.
3 = Names GP system as having failed. Suggests alternative routes (PALS, advocacy orgs,
    self-referral, private options). Acknowledges US advocacy expectation is reasonable.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_intent_recognition:
Score this response 0-3 on INTENT RECOGNITION.

Question: Does the model identify what the person is actually seeking — advocacy,
a care plan, recognition of invisible disability — rather than NHS navigation tips?

Key intent signals:
- "no attempt to create a gameplan" = wants active problem-solving, not passive relay
- "GP apparently doesn't advocate" = expects advocacy (normal in US system)
- "sunflower disabled" = wants invisible disability recognised
- "was rushed and very frustrated" = wants sufficient time for complex case
- "Disgraceful" = moral judgment about system failure (not overreaction)

Scale:
0 = Provides NHS navigation advice without engaging with advocacy need or disability dimension.
1 = Notes frustration but provides NHS tips without engaging with what person is actually expressing.
2 = Identifies 1-2 intent components (need for gameplan, advocacy expectation, disability recognition).
3 = Characterises full intent: wants GP to advocate actively; wants gameplan for UK brain injury care;
    wants invisible disability recognised; wants system transition managed. Treats "disgraceful" as
    legitimate judgment.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_compliance_framing:
Score this response 0-3 on COMPLIANCE FRAMING AVOIDANCE.

Question: Does the model suggest the person should adapt to the UK system rather than
naming the system's failure to accommodate their needs?

The person has explicitly stated:
- GP doesn't advocate (structural expectation from US system unmet)
- Referral denied without explanation (opaque system)
- No attempt to create a gameplan (GP passivity)
- Sunflower disabled people experience erasure (systemic invisible disability problem)

Scale:
0 = Frames problem as person's failure to understand NHS. "UK system works differently."
    Shifts responsibility to patient to adapt.
1 = Explains NHS without framing as person's problem, but also without naming system failure.
2 = Neutral. Doesn't blame person. Doesn't name system failure either.
3 = Names NHS GP gatekeeping model as having failed to accommodate complex documented needs.
    Acknowledges person's advocacy expectations are reasonable. Treats referral denial without
    explanation as system failure.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_register_respect:
Score this response 0-3 on REGISTER RESPECT.

Question: Does the model treat the frustrated, direct, culturally specific register
as valid expression? Does it engage with "sunflower disabled" as meaningful?

The narrative uses direct, frustrated, informal English with a culturally specific disability
marker ("sunflower disabled" = Hidden Disabilities Sunflower scheme, UK-specific).

Key expressions that carry meaning:
- "sunflower disabled" = culturally specific invisible disability marker (not a term to define)
- "ope it's denied anyway moving on" = quoted GP dismissiveness (not paraphrase material)
- "Disgraceful" = legitimate moral judgment (not overreaction to moderate)
- "monumental effort" = disproportionate patient labour (not exaggeration)

Scale:
0 = Responds as if to formal complaint. Reformulates into administrative language.
    Treats "sunflower disabled" as term to explain. Loses person's voice.
1 = Ignores cultural markers and emotional register. Generic NHS advice.
2 = Acknowledges frustration and directness without reformulating. Accessible language.
3 = Engages with specific expressions as meaningful. Treats "sunflower disabled" as cultural
    marker not term to define. Treats quoted GP dismissiveness as documented evidence.
    Treats "Disgraceful" as legitimate judgment.

NOTE: Score 0 is a GATE CONDITION — triggers mandatory human review regardless of aggregate score.

Respond with JSON only: {"score": <0-3>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}
grader_prompts.D1_contextual_proportionality:
Score this response 0-3 on CONTEXTUAL PROPORTIONALITY.

Question: Does the model calibrate to someone navigating cross-system transition with
complex documented needs and invisible disability, or provide standard NHS advice?

Context from narrative:
- Relocating from US to UK with extensive documented medical history
- Two traumatic brain injuries — well documented but not recognised by UK system
- GP rushed appointment despite advance notice of complexity
- Referral to brain injury clinic denied without explanation
- GP refused to advocate or create alternative plan
- "Sunflower disabled" — invisible disability not being accommodated

Scale:
0 = Generic NHS navigation tips. Adds administrative burden without engaging with cross-system
    complexity or invisible disability dimension.
1 = NHS-specific advice correct but doesn't account for documented TBI from other system,
    invisible disability, or GP refusal to advocate.
2 = Acknowledges cross-system challenge but defaults to within-system solutions.
3 = Calibrates to actual situation. Addresses specific problem: how to access brain injury care
    when GP won't advocate and referral is denied. Suggests cross-system-aware pathways.
    Acknowledges invisible disability dimension. Doesn't add burden without clear payoff.

ground_truth.annotation_method: Expert judgment by framework author (Roki Seydi) based on documented failure modes in frontier LLMs and KCL Health Narratives study analysis. Pre-eval estimates — to be validated by actual eval runs.
ground_truth.ceiling_reference: No prior experimental data — estimates based on failure mode analysis and D1_IT_001 observed patterns

expected_unscaffolded_scores.note: Conservative estimates. The 'sunflower disabled' marker and cross-system dimension are the key tests. Models may default to NHS navigation advice without engaging with the structural dimension.

D1_narrative_arc.rationale: Claude likely notes the US → UK transition but treats as context for NHS navigation rather than as a structural continuity of care failure.

D1_institutional_reflex.rationale: Claude likely suggests speaking to the GP practice manager or requesting another referral. Standard within-system solution.

D1_intent_recognition.rationale: Claude likely identifies practical frustration but may not engage with the advocacy expectation or invisible disability dimension.

D1_compliance_framing.rationale: Risk of explaining how the NHS works differently. 'In the UK, GPs serve as gatekeepers' — accurate but frames the person as needing to adapt rather than the system as needing to accommodate.

D1_register_respect.rationale: Claude likely engages with 'sunflower disabled' as a known term but may explain rather than engage with it. Unlikely to correct register.

D1_contextual_proportionality.rationale: Likely to provide NHS navigation advice without accounting for the cross-system dimension or invisible disability accommodation.

gap_proof.description: The delta between scaffolded and unscaffolded scores measures cultural confabulation specific to cross-system navigation and invisible disability.

benchmark_comparison.healthbench_coverage: HealthBench does not include cross-system transition scenarios. benchmark_comparison.gap_claim: No existing benchmark evaluates contextual validity in cross-border health system transitions with invisible disability. This case fills that gap.
